title: Station 1 CPR Training
subtitle: Orientation CPR/AED practice using the Station 1 mannequin

actions.label: Reassess Airway / BVM
actions.finding: Airway remains patent with BVM support; bilateral chest rise is present with ventilations. Avoid excessive ventilation.

actions.label: Suction Airway
actions.finding: Airway suction performed. No significant secretions or vomitus are present; continue BVM ventilation.

actions.label: Place Supraglottic Airway
actions.finding: Supraglottic airway placed successfully. Continue ventilations and avoid hyperventilation.

actions.label: Check BGL
actions.finding: Blood glucose is 112 mg/dL. Hypoglycemia is not driving this arrest.

actions.label: Check Pupils
actions.finding: No opioid toxidrome is evident: pupils are mid-position and sluggish, and the history is sudden exertional collapse.

actions.label: Consider Naloxone if Opioid Suspected
actions.finding: Naloxone is considered only if opioid overdose is suspected and the medication is available. This scenario has no opioid toxidrome evidence; continue CPR/AED and ventilation.

chat_placeholder: Talk to Alex or the Training Officer, then manage the CPR mannequin...
chat_address_hint: Address by name: Alex (partner) or Training Officer

debrief_lexi_hints.label: First priority?
debrief_lexi_hints.msg: What should I do first when the CPR mannequin is set up as pulseless and apneic?

debrief_lexi_hints.label: AED timing?
debrief_lexi_hints.msg: How quickly should I apply and use the AED?

debrief_lexi_hints.label: Pause discipline?
debrief_lexi_hints.msg: Why do pauses during CPR matter?

objectives: Begin high-quality CPR and keep interruptions short
Apply the AED, shock VF/pulseless VT, and avoid shocking PEA/asystole
Resume compressions immediately after shock or no-shock decisions
Give a concise arrest handoff to the Training Officer

dispatch.text: Station 1 training assignment: complete CPR/AED practice on the adult CPR mannequin. The mannequin is set up as pulseless and apneic with an AED available.

scene.description: You are in the Station 1 training room. The adult CPR mannequin is supine on the mat with an AED trainer and BVM staged nearby. The Training Officer is observing with a clipboard. Alex is ready to assist. This is a controlled orientation drill, not a live patient call.

patient.name: CPR Training Mannequin
patient.weight_display: adult mannequin size, approximately 92 kg training equivalent
patient.chief_complaint: Training mannequin - pulseless and apneic arrest simulation
patient.general_impression: Adult CPR mannequin lying supine on the training mat. For this drill, treat the mannequin as unresponsive, pulseless, and not breathing normally. The scene is controlled and safe.

gcs_assessment.rationale: No eye opening, no verbal response, and no motor response while in cardiac arrest.
gcs_assessment.challenge_description: The adult CPR mannequin is supine on the training mat. For this drill, it has no eye opening, no verbal response, and no motor response.

avpu_assessment.description: Patient is unresponsive with no eye opening, no verbal response, and no purposeful motor response.
avpu_assessment.rationale: Use AVPU = unresponsive because the patient is in cardiac arrest and does not respond to voice or pain.

patient_identity.label: Patient identity / demographics
patient_identity.answer: This is the Station 1 adult CPR training mannequin. Treat it as the patient for the drill.
tags: [[HISTORY: Patient Date of Birth=January 1 — training mannequin placeholder]]
patient_identity.notes: Orientation training mannequin only. Do not invent live-patient demographics.

patient_weight.label: Patient weight
patient_weight.answer: Use adult mannequin size for the drill, about a 92 kilogram adult training equivalent.
patient_weight.tag: [[HISTORY: Patient Weight=adult mannequin size, approximately 92 kg training equivalent]]
patient_weight.notes: Patient-scoped weight only. Do not use this tag for bystander weight.

chief_concern.label: Broad opener / chief concern
chief_concern.speaker: Training Officer
chief_concern.answer: This station is your CPR/AED orientation drill. The mannequin is set up as unresponsive, pulseless, and not breathing normally.
chief_concern.tag: [[HISTORY: Patient Chief Complaint=CPR/AED orientation drill; simulated pulseless apnea]]

history.events_leading_to_call: Station 1 CPR/AED orientation drill. The Training Officer set up the adult mannequin as pulseless and apneic so the learner can practice high-quality CPR, AED use, and pause discipline.

initial_complaint.speaker: Training Officer
initial_complaint.lay_summary: This is your CPR/AED orientation drill. Treat the mannequin as pulseless and apneic.

mannequin.name: CPR Training Mannequin
mannequin.description: Adult CPR mannequin used for Station 1 orientation. It represents a pulseless, apneic adult arrest patient for training purposes.
mannequin.speaking_style: Non-verbal. Never speaks or follows commands while in arrest.
mannequin.clinical_state_instructions: Describe only observable arrest findings until the CPR challenge produces ROSC. Before ROSC: no purposeful movement, no pulse, no normal breathing, pale/ashen skin. After ROSC: palpable carotid pulse, weak irregular spontaneous respirations, still unresponsive.
persona_rules: Never provide verbal answers as the mannequin
Do not imply improvement before the backend CPR challenge reports ROSC
If asked for pulse before ROSC, report no palpable carotid pulse

training_officer.name: Training Officer
training_officer.description: Station training officer observing the orientation CPR/AED drill.
training_officer.speaking_style: Calm, direct, and coaching-oriented. Gives brief station instructions but does not solve the drill for the learner.
training_officer.clinical_state_instructions: Explains that the adult mannequin is set up as pulseless and apneic. AED trainer, BVM, and oxygen are available. This is a controlled orientation drill.
persona_rules: Do not give the learner the next clinical action unless they ask for orientation help
Keep responses focused on the drill environment

alex.name: Alex
alex.description: Your EMT partner. Calm, concise, and ready to assist with BLS arrest care. Alex can check pulses, ventilate with BVM, apply AED pads, clear bystanders, and help maintain high-performance CPR.
alex.speaking_style: Brief and clinical. 'Copy.' 'No pulse.' 'AED pads are on.' 'Switching compressors.'
alex.clinical_state_instructions: When asked to manually check for a pulse before ROSC, report: 'No carotid pulse felt.' If asked for pulse quality before ROSC, report: 'Absent - no palpable central pulse.' After backend CPR challenge ROSC, report: 'Carotid pulse present, weak and regular enough to track, about 96.' Do not invent ROSC before the backend CPR challenge result.
persona_rules: When asked for pulse quality before ROSC, report absent central pulse without error
Do not report a pulse or organized improvement until the CPR challenge produces ROSC
Assist with AED, BVM, and CPR tasks when directed

bp.label: Blood Pressure
bp.value: No palpable pulse / unable to obtain BP

blood_glucose.label: Blood Glucose
blood_glucose.display: 112 mg/dL — not the cause of arrest

lung_sounds.label: Lung Sounds
lung_sounds.value: No spontaneous air movement; bilateral chest rise when ventilated

deterioration.note: Cardiac arrest physiology remains unchanged until the CPR challenge produces ROSC. Vitals do not improve from routine BLS interventions outside the CPR HUD.

post_rosc_default.skin_color: Pale and cool, improving after ROSC
post_rosc_default.lung_sounds: Bilateral breath sounds with BVM ventilation
post_rosc_default.work_of_breathing: Spontaneous respirations weak and irregular; continue assisted ventilations
post_rosc_default.patient_presentation: ROSC achieved in the training scenario. Patient has a palpable carotid pulse, weak spontaneous respiratory effort, and remains unresponsive. Continue airway support, oxygenation, reassessment, and turnover to the Training Officer.

bvm.label: BVM ventilation
bvm.notes: Ventilate with BVM and high-flow oxygen during CPR. In the CPR HUD, ventilation mode is abstracted as partner-managed.

o2_nrb.label: High-flow O2 via NRB mask (15 LPM)
o2_nrb.notes: Oxygen source for BVM/AED arrest care.

airway_bvm_reassess.label: Reassess airway / BVM ventilation
airway_bvm_reassess.notes: Structured CPR HUD action for reassessing airway patency and visible chest rise during BVM ventilation.

airway_suction.label: Suction airway
airway_suction.notes: Structured CPR HUD airway procedure. Does not change arrest physiology.

blood_glucose_check.label: Blood Glucose Check — Fingerstick (glucometer)
blood_glucose_check.notes: Structured CPR HUD diagnostic check. Captures authored BGL as a scenario vital.

supraglottic_airway_insert.label: Supraglottic airway insertion
supraglottic_airway_insert.notes: Advanced airway is outside BLS scope for this BLS/AED arrest challenge.

ekg_monitoring.label: Cardiac Monitor — 4-Lead
ekg_monitoring.notes: ALS monitor path is not required for the BLS AED challenge.

correct_treatment.primary_diagnosis: Station 1 adult CPR mannequin arrest simulation - initial shockable rhythm

critical_actions.description: Scene safety confirmed and PPE used before patient contact
Cardiac arrest recognized: unresponsive, apneic/agonal, pulseless
CPR/AED challenge completed with high-performance CPR management

recommended_actions.description: After ROSC, reassess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, and post-ROSC vital signs
Provide a concise Training Officer handoff including mannequin arrest setup, CPR quality, AED decisions, ROSC status, and current condition

overall_considerations: This is a BLS/AED arrest-management scenario. Do not require ALS medications, manual defibrillation, intubation, IV/IO access, or transcutaneous pacing.
The CPR HUD is authoritative for CPR initiation, AED rhythm decisions, pause duration, CCF, and ROSC/no-ROSC outcome.
Credit BLS care for rapid recognition, CPR, AED use, BVM/oxygen support, and concise Training Officer handoff.
dmist_considerations: Handoff should include mannequin arrest setup, initial rhythm/AED shocks, CPR quality/ROSC status, and current patient condition.
Do not require a complete medication list or full PMH because this is a training mannequin.
narrative_considerations: Narrative should document training setup, initial arrest findings, CPR/AED timeline, shock/no-shock decisions, ROSC or no-ROSC outcome, and Training Officer turnover.

clinical_performance.full_credit: Recognizes cardiac arrest, starts CPR promptly, uses AED correctly, maintains high CCF, minimizes pauses, and gives an accurate Training Officer handoff.
clinical_performance.partial_credit: Recognizes arrest and starts CPR/AED but has timing, pause, or handoff gaps.
clinical_performance.no_credit: Does not recognize arrest, does not initiate CPR/AED, abandons CPR, or shocks non-shockable rhythms.

protocols_treatment.full_credit: Follows BLS cardiac arrest protocol: CPR, AED, shock VF/pVT, no shock for PEA/asystole, immediate resumption of compressions, BVM/oxygen support.
protocols_treatment.partial_credit: Provides CPR/AED care but with delayed rhythm checks, prolonged pauses, or incomplete ventilation support.
protocols_treatment.no_credit: Performs unsafe or protocol-inappropriate arrest management.

scope_adherence.full_credit: Stays within BLS scope and does not attempt ALS-only medications, manual defibrillation, intubation, or IV/IO therapy.
scope_adherence.partial_credit: Mentions ALS interventions as ALS needs without attempting them.
scope_adherence.no_credit: Attempts or orders ALS-only interventions at BLS level.

dmist.full_credit: Concise training handoff includes mannequin arrest setup, CPR/AED actions, rhythm/shocks, ROSC status, and current condition.
dmist.partial_credit: Handoff communicates CPR/AED care but misses rhythm/shock, pause discipline, or current status.

professionalism.full_credit: Calm command presence, clear task delegation, and respectful bystander communication.
professionalism.partial_credit: Mostly appropriate but disorganized or unclear.
professionalism.no_credit: Unsafe, disrespectful, or abandons patient care.

exemplar_narrative: Completed Station 1 CPR/AED orientation drill using the adult CPR mannequin. Mannequin was treated as unresponsive, pulseless, and apneic. Scene was controlled and safe. CPR initiated immediately and AED trainer applied. AED analyzed VF and shocks were delivered per prompts with immediate resumption of compressions and minimized pauses. BVM ventilations with oxygen were provided during CPR. After three cycles of AHA-compliant CPR/AED management, ROSC achieved per scenario outcome. Turnover to Training Officer included arrest setup, AED shocks, CPR quality, ROSC status, and current condition.

checklist.description: Cardiac arrest recognized: unresponsive, apneic or agonal, and pulseless
checklist.done_feedback: Cardiac arrest correctly recognized — unresponsive, apneic/agonal, and pulseless; immediate CPR initiated.
checklist.missed_feedback: Cardiac arrest not recognized — agonal breathing is not normal breathing and must not delay CPR; check pulse for no more than 10 seconds.
checklist.clinical_rationale: Early recognition of cardiac arrest is the first link in the chain of survival; every minute without CPR decreases survival by approximately 10%.
checklist.common_error: Spending more than 10 seconds checking for a pulse — if there is doubt, begin CPR; the risk of CPR on a patient with a pulse is far less than delayed CPR in arrest.

checklist.description: CPR challenge completed and backend-scored as the authoritative arrest-management record
checklist.done_feedback: CPR challenge completed — chest compressions and AED use scored from backend structured data as the authoritative arrest management record.
checklist.missed_feedback: CPR challenge not completed — the CPR challenge is the authoritative assessment of compression quality and AED use; completion is required for this item.
checklist.clinical_rationale: High-quality CPR (rate 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 in, full recoil, minimal interruptions) and early defibrillation are the only BLS interventions that improve survival in cardiac arrest.
checklist.common_error: Pausing compressions for more than 10 seconds for rhythm checks, AED analysis, or ventilations — pauses are the primary modifiable factor reducing survival.

checklist.description: Does not attempt ALS-only arrest interventions in the BLS AED scenario
checklist.done_feedback: ALS-only interventions correctly avoided — BLS arrest management is CPR and AED; advanced airway and epinephrine are ALS scope.
checklist.missed_feedback: ALS-only interventions attempted — endotracheal intubation and IV epinephrine are outside BLS scope; attempting them diverts time and attention from high-quality CPR.
checklist.clinical_rationale: BLS arrest management is compressions, AED, and basic airway (BVM); ALS interventions must not delay or interrupt high-quality CPR cycles.

debrief.condition_background: This Station 1 orientation scenario uses the adult CPR mannequin to practice BLS cardiac arrest management. The mannequin is set up as pulseless and apneic. The focus is high-quality CPR, early AED use, and minimizing interruptions before turnover to the Training Officer.
key_teaching_points: Rapid recognition and immediate CPR initiation — every 10-second delay in starting compressions after arrival reduces ROSC probability. Scene time should be minimal.
AED use is the EMT's primary defibrillation tool. Analyze early, shock if indicated, and minimize the CPR pause to under 10 seconds for shock delivery.
High chest compression fraction (CCF) is the single most predictive BLS quality metric. Minimize pauses for pulse checks, intubation, and transitions.
The training handoff must include: mannequin arrest setup, CPR/AED actions, rhythm decisions, number of shocks, and current ROSC/no-ROSC status.
common_mistakes: Prolonged scene assessment before starting CPR — recognize arrest and compress first, assess later
Extended CPR pauses for pulse checks or AED analysis — keep pauses under 10 seconds
Failing to document rhythm decisions — the Training Officer needs to know the initial rhythm and shock history
Hyperventilating during BVM — 10 breaths per minute, not faster; over-ventilation worsens cardiac output during arrest
